Deputy David Cullinane  Obviously, there has to be an element of cross-departmental and cross-ministerial work on workforce planning. The health service cannot recruit quickly enough if we are not training and training capacity is very important. I imagine a lot of collaborative work is being done between the Ministers and the Departments. What I am saying is that I will raise this with the Minister for higher education-----
Deputy Jennifer Carroll MacNeill  Yes, and I will too.

Deputy David Cullinane  -----but I am raising it with the Minister for Health also. We could also have more training capacity in the south east and get the benefit from SETU and its plans. The hospital manager, whom the Minister has praised for other matters, sees this as an exciting opportunity.  The 24-7 cardiology care was a big day for Waterford and is a big step forward for health in the south east. I commend the Minister on the fact she ensured this happened. There was a campaign over a long number of years. I also commend people in Waterford and the south east, including in Carlow, Kilkenny, Wexford and Tipperary, who came out in big numbers on a number of occasions to demand equality of access. The fact it is now there is good news for everybody in the south east.

Deputy Jennifer Carroll MacNeill  I thank Deputy Cullinane and I acknowledge all of the people involved. The impact of it means people can rest easier knowing they will have the care they need at any time they need it. Of course I will discuss it with the Minister, Deputy Lawless, and of course it is collaborative. The more I think about the co-location of hospitals and medical schools, what we are trying to do in medical devices and pharmaceuticals, and the opportunity for research and innovation, it is driving health not just for the benefit of patients but to make sure it is a driver for the economy as well. We have such an opportunity and we have such a talented workforce. We have such a medical clinical profession that is dedicated to research and innovation. All of these opportunities present and I am so glad Deputy Cullinane has raised SETU in this context as well.
Health Services Staff

Deputy Marie Sherlock  My question relates to delayed transfers of care and nursing home capacity. I understand that yesterday marked the highest number of delayed transfers of care across our acute hospitals in the year to date, with 555 patients who should have been discharged and were not. This marks 86 continuous days of over 500 delayed transfers of care in our hospitals. For the first four months of this year, almost 50% of those waiting to leave hospital were waiting for a nursing home place. What is the response to this crisis?

Deputy Jennifer Carroll MacNeill  I thank Deputy Sherlock, who is quite right to highlight delayed transfer of care, DTOC. As she said, it has been well over 500 variously for the last period, which is essentially a hospital. This is why the use of every bed is so very important. Deputy Sherlock is correct to identify nursing home beds, community nursing units and all of the appropriate step-down facilities that are, can and should be in the system to make sure we do not have people staying in acute hospitals longer than is necessary.  In this context, the national service plan commits to the delivery of 352 community beds in residential settings for older people in this period. The community nursing unit capital programme is ongoing and will have 90 public community nursing units and community hospital beds refurbished or replaced so they meet all of the regulatory requirements. It was a big problem for us that we had beds that were closed because of HIQA standards. This is correct because we have to bring them up to standard but it is a body of work to do this. The public-private partnership project will deliver 528 residential care beds on seven sites in 2026. The programme for Government commits to building more public nursing home beds, including dementia-specific provision, which is a huge issue in the delayed transfer of care.  We are developing a new long-term residential care additional capacity plan, which will be published in the later part of this year. There is a complexity to many of the DTOCs. There is an increase in complexity, particularly on the dementia side. There are other cases where there is a clear pathway out of hospital. I have highlighted the south west a number of times because of my frustration with the use and non-use of Mallow. There are patients in hospital today who could be discharged to virtual beds that are not being used. I do not need to conflate these different issues but it speaks to efficiency and the management of every bed and seeing every bed as being so very important.

Deputy Marie Sherlock  The average waiting time in DTOC is 24.8 days across all acute hospitals and in the midlands and north-east Dublin it is more than 30 days. This is a huge cause for alarm in terms of risk to the patients themselves and their prospects of recovery but also the cost to the hospital. We know that nursing home demand is the biggest driver of this, with more than 1,500 people waiting for a nursing home space, but I have to ask about the urgency with regard to the Government's response. In 2022, ESRI research identified that we would need somewhere between 54,000 and 60,000 nursing home beds. We had 33,000 beds back in 2022. We have gone backwards and in 2025 we had fewer beds at 32,408. We are not even running to stand still. I am glad to hear a capacity expansion plan will be put in place but, like the acute hospital expansion plan, it has been sitting there for a number of years and I am not hearing urgency with regard to this problem.

Deputy Jennifer Carroll MacNeill  Going back a couple of days, on 6 July there were 526 delayed transfers of care. Of these, 68 involved legal complexity, 35 involved basic housing needs, 90 involved very complex clinical needs, 61 were about access to rehab facilities of various kinds, home support packages were involved in 42 cases, residential care in 217 cases and non-compliance in 13 cases. It is not just about nursing home support; it is also about support at home. For example, it is important to mention the expansion of the enhanced community care teams, which are the multidisciplinary teams supporting earlier discharge from hospital closer to home. There is also extended service availability in the evenings and at weekends, including discharge planning, community supports and decision makers addressing avoidable non-clinical delays to discharge where it is possible.  I have mentioned the virtual wards. I do not suggest that everybody would move onto a virtual ward but there are people for whom it is clinically appropriate to do so. Crucially, the SAFER patient flow bundle and the plan for every patient are now in place across most sites, supported by seven-day and 14-day length-of-stay reviews.

Deputy Marie Sherlock  Obviously, there are a whole range of issues as to why people do not leave hospital. Almost 70% of the cases relate to a need for residential care or complex care such as dementia. The big issue is the cost. We know the HSE is running a significant deficit at the moment. It will not even make the savings it is supposed to make at the end of this year. We know the cost of an acute daybed is about €1,300, which is almost five times the cost of a community nursing unit bed. This has a very real financial implication for hospitals. The question being asked of me is about the tolerance and particularly the very long average waiting times across our acute hospitals. We need to see a much greater urgency with regard to a nursing home capacity plan, which would be a public nursing home capacity plan.

Deputy Jennifer Carroll MacNeill  I agree with Deputy Sherlock. I recall speaking with one of the hospital managers where this is a most acute issue with regard to what is going on, what the experience is and what conversations are happening. Again, there is a question of dementia and advanced decision-making. Some of these issues are very complex and that is its own bundle. As Deputy Sherlock said, there is also the need to access nursing homes and public nursing home support. Within this, it is important to say we will not be able to facilitate every request for every patient to be in the exact location they need to be. There are some questions. I do not wish to overstate it or amplify it but I am saying we will be able to provide supports but we will not be able to provide the perfect support for every person either. A measure of flexibility will have to come into that too.  There are also cases, and again it is hospital managers describing this to me, where families do not want to have a particular outcome and the hospital needs to move the patient, who is no longer an acute model 4 hospital patient but requires a different type of care.  It is important that everybody understands that model 4 beds are for the most acutely unwell and people cannot stay.
Nursing Homes

Deputy Martin Daly  I ask the Minister for an update on the plan to increase the capacity of inpatient beds by 4,000 or 4,500 across the country and if she will make a statement on the matter. We know we currently have 12,000 public beds and 2,500 private beds, which is about 2.9 beds per thousand of the population. We also know that the ESRI has predicted a need for a 40% increase in inpatient beds by 2040 and that the plan in 2024 was to provide 3,352 new beds.

Deputy Jennifer Carroll MacNeill  I thank the Deputy. The programme for Government, as the Deputy said, sets out a commitment to increase bed capacity by between 4,000 and 4,500 new and refurbished inpatient hospital beds across the country. This will be achieved through a regionally balanced capacity expansion plan. The substantially enhanced national development plan, NDP, provision for the health sector of €9.25 billion will support the delivery of those beds.  To give the Deputy a much more specific update, 292 new acute inpatient beds have now opened across the country since the programme for Government was agreed, between January 2025 and June 2026, including 128 beds in University Hospital Limerick, 18 beds in Cork University Hospital, 15 beds in Our Lady of Lourdes Hospital, Drogheda, 20 beds in Beaumont Hospital and 24 beds in Mallow General Hospital. The HSE anticipates opening 108 more new beds over the course of quarter 3 and quarter 4 of 2026 and quarter 1 of 2027.  Some 259 inpatient beds are currently under construction, including 97 beds at Wexford General Hospital, 42 beds at Sligo University Hospital and 30 beds in University Hospital Kerry. It is intended to open those beds as soon as possible once constructed. In fact, as the Deputy is aware, we are recruiting ahead of the beds being delivered to make sure the staff are there. A further 185 beds are at tender stage and another 152 beds are now at the detailed design stage. We are now seeing this bed-by-bed, significant pipeline of projects progressing through earlier stages of planning, design and approvals. Rather than giving the Deputy broad figures, I thought I would be very specific as to the progress being achieved.

Deputy Martin Daly  I thank the Minister very much. We had the excellent Professor Rose Anne Kenny, co-ordinator of The Irish Longitudinal Study on Ageing, TILDA, in recently with the health committee. She pointed out the demographic challenge we are facing in health. We are going to have 1 million people over the age of 65 by 2030 and a doubling of the number of those aged over 65 by 2057, to 1.6 million people. We have already seen this trend happening from 2013, with an increase from 569,000 to 806,000.  I bring these figures up because this challenge is looming. Obviously, beds are not a silver bullet, but they are going to be part of the overall way we manage health in future. We will probably start with proper screening programmes at the age of 50, with the whole population moving into better-resourced primary care, step-down beds and rehabilitation beds. I also support Deputy Seán Kyne’s call for the Minister to press for the 300 additional beds in UHG. This is a level 4 hospital operating with level 3 capacity.

Deputy Jennifer Carroll MacNeill  I thank the Deputy. He will not hear any complaint from me about people pressing me to deliver more health infrastructure or to spend more capital moneys. I am doing my very best to spend my entire capital allocation as quickly as possible and I run the risk of spending other people’s capital allocation, if I can be so lucky or so bold. This is because, as the Deputy said, this infrastructure investment is essential for the future.  While I do not wish to be flippant about it and, of course, we must achieve value for money and we must take the correct decisions through the right processes, we really have to aggressively spend on health infrastructure in the right way to make sure we are going to meet the very excellent challenge of having not just a growing population but an ageing population. Some of my European health colleagues do not have that same wonderful situation. Nevertheless, it creates a significant infrastructure challenge for us for the future.

Deputy Martin Daly  I accept that answer and I thank the Minister very much for that update. It is important because people sometimes equate healthcare with hospital beds, but that is not reality. In isolation, beds cannot provide for the healthcare needs of the public. We will never develop enough capacity in beds unless we change the way we manage healthcare, both in preventative and in primary care. We really do have to step up the number of rehabilitation beds, step-down beds, home care packages, primary care therapies and nursing home beds in the community. We also need to think more strategically about how we manage the health of the population from the age of 50 onwards. Professor Rose Anne Kenny made that very point to the health committee. Deputy Sherlock already pointed out this challenge because we both listened to Professor Kenny's excellent presentation at the health committee last week.

Deputy Jennifer Carroll MacNeill  I agree, particularly concerning rehab beds, step-down beds and neurorehab beds. I was in Blarney with Deputy Colm Burke to look at the neurorehab step-down beds in Mercy University Hospital. It was quite creatively done. It was a former hotel on a golf course that was acquired and redesigned for this purpose.  We really are trying to be imaginative and innovative about where we can acquire space to be able to provide beds of every kind. I was in Tallaght University Hospital yesterday, where existing space has been found within the hospital to be repurposed for beds. I have been in hospitals where there are plans to turn records rooms, where patient records have been stored, into beds. Hospital managers are being creative where they can be at the moment, but none of those things are going to equate to the scale of the number of beds we need. We need this significant capital programme, which I hope I have outlined. I do really recognise the challenge.
Hospital Facilities

Deputy Shane Moynihan  These questions are to ask the Minister for Health what steps her Department is taking to address the delays in processing reimbursement applications submitted under the cross-border directive. Will reimbursement under the cross-border directive include interest costs incurred on bank loans or credit facilities used by patients to fund upfront medical treatment abroad, particularly in those circumstances where the reimbursement delays significantly exceed indicative processing timelines?

Deputy Jennifer Carroll MacNeill  I propose to take Questions Nos. 96 and 98 together.  The cross-border directive allows public patients to access healthcare, which they would have been entitled to access in the public healthcare service in Ireland, in another EU or EEA country. As the Deputy is aware, patients pay upfront for the treatment and are reimbursed upon their return to Ireland. We want to support patients in this way and I do not want them to have additional administrative or any form of burden because of the operation of this scheme.  The HSE is responsible for the operation of the cross-border directive and has a dedicated office for that purpose. There has been continued growth in use of the scheme. The HSE tells me that this has placed additional demands on the HSE cross-border directive, CBD, office and given rise to a build-up of applications. The increased activity is evidenced by the number of treatments reimbursed increasing by approximately 75% between 2020 and 2024.  Nevertheless, it is important this service operates in a responsive way and that reimbursement applications are processed within a reasonable timeframe. The HSE advises that it usually aims to repay patients within 30 days of receipt of all completed documents. However, the HSE also advises that it is currently processing applications received in mid-January 2026. A six-month delay for reimbursement is absolutely unacceptable. It is not fair to patients to have an additional financial burden placed on them that is occasioned simply because of administrative delay, notwithstanding the increase in applications.  Accordingly, the HSE has advised my Department that additional resources are being assigned to manage this increase in demand with the aim of restoring processing times back to expected and more acceptable levels. I have, however, set the HSE a very specific target of getting back to the 30-day target by the end of 2026. It will need to sort this issue out between now and then and get back to that 30-day target. It is not reasonable to put administrative stress, financial stress or other burdens on people in addition to their healthcare issues.  As the Deputy knows, the reimbursement amount is the cost of the treatment in Ireland or the cost of it abroad, whichever is the lesser. Reimbursement is confined to the costs of the care itself and does not include any additional costs incurred by the patient. This is not the Deputy's question, however. His question is about the timeline, and I hope I have given him some confidence that this will be changed by the HSE.

Deputy Shane Moynihan  I thank the Minister for that very comprehensive answer. I welcome her support and proactiveness in getting the HSE to commit to that timeline.  I have a few follow-up questions. This is around confidence-building. It is a very useful directive and gives us additional capacity in getting people the treatment they need. From my understanding of how the scheme operates, it centres on a small number of providers and hospitals because of the specific procedures that are there. As the Minister said, waiting six months for reimbursement is onerous, especially if it is an older relative or someone who has been waiting for a long time.  Could the Minister say a bit more about whether any consideration can be given to a situation where a family, not of substantial means, has had to take a loan to pay for the overall cost of the treatment and because they have not been able to repay that loan, or because the reimbursement has been delayed, this has led to interest being accrued on the credit facility they took?  Is the HSE considering providing alleviation for families who find themselves in those circumstances through, as the Minister said, no fault of their own? It is unacceptable they should have to wait that duration of time to receive the reimbursement due to them and they then have to pay an additional interest or credit charge on top of that. There is a question of fairness. I fully appreciate and am grateful to the Minister for driving the HSE to get back to a 30-day target by the end of 2026. How confident is the Minister that it will do that? Is it allocating additional staff? What exactly does additional resource look like? Will the HSE report to the Minister with an interim progress report before the end of the year to see how this is tracking? Has the HSE made any substantial progress in addressing that target in the meantime?  It is a good directive and scheme. From a political point of view, we are very supportive of this but it is also about making sure patients relying on it get the reimbursement they are due. As I am sure the Minister will agree, we do not want to cause more hardship to or place onerous pressure on people who may have been through a procedure or have had a loved one go through a procedure. Will the Minister share her thoughts on the staffing, her confidence that the HSE will reach the target and the refund of interest on credit facilities?

Deputy Jennifer Carroll MacNeill  I cannot speak to the specific staff numbers. I generally find it is about process change as much as staff increases, so I think it is a measure of both. I will look for an interim report, probably in October, as to how the work is going. I made the point that it is possible to change processes. For example, this time last year, I gave the Medical Council a significant challenge to sort out its backlog of people applying to be doctors in this country. It was a patient safety issue not to have had them. By September, it had that done. These things can be done with a measure of focus, and I expect it to be done in this case.  In relation to the specific question about interest and bank loans, there is a difficulty with this. The reimbursement is limited to the eligible healthcare costs that would have been met by the public health service. There is no provision for the reimbursement of interest charges, borrowing costs or other consequential financial costs. Reimbursement under the cross-border directive is governed by European and national legislation. Reimbursement of additional costs is not envisaged by the directive because it was never expected people would be waiting in that period. I appreciate the unfairness point the Deputy made. That is why I am saying these delays are absolutely unacceptable and we need to get back but there is not provision in that scheme. We have no flexibility to change a European scheme of that kind in relation to additional costs beyond the healthcare costs.

Deputy Shane Moynihan  I thank the Minister for her response, in particular on the process change. If possible, will the Minister tell us if there are particular processes the HSE has identified which it thinks could be changed to speed this up? I am always reluctant to say we need to throw more staff at a problem, especially when it is administrative, because, as the Minister said, more often than not a system change is needed or there are unnecessary barriers in processing which cause delays.  I fully accept the Minister's point that the directive is European legislation but the European legislation did not envisage it would take six months to reimburse an application. In that vein, is there any consideration that can be given internally for the HSE to look at what it might to do remediate and compensate the people who, through no fault of their own, are left to pay large amounts of interest they did not envisage when they took out the loans? That was not an expectation of the scheme when it was set up. It certainly was not the expectation of the patients when they applied for the scheme but, with all due respect to all involved, the HSE is the one at fault for the delay. There should be some indication or signal from it about how it might compensate or at least take account of the fact that the interest accrued or credit facilities people took have given rise to additional bank charges on the fees.  From a human point of view, families who have gone through the stress of having to travel abroad to get a procedure are expecting reimbursement. Six months down the line, they have not been reimbursed and are hit with extra interest or bank charges. This was not envisaged by the European legislation. I fully accept the point on flexibility and the parameters but given that this is a process issue related to the administering of this scheme, will the HSE look at compensating for or taking account of that in the reimbursement process?

Deputy Jennifer Carroll MacNeill  I appreciate the point the Deputy is making but I am not going to make any commitments or policy on the floor of the House. I am also operating without information on the specific patients, the duration or the interest costs. I do not have any of that information but if the Deputy wants to speak to me about the matter privately, I will be happy to look at it. However, I will not make promises I cannot deliver as it would not be right for me to do so. I appreciate the Deputy's point and his reasoning.
Question No. 97 taken with Written Answers.
Question No. 98 taken with Question No. 96.
EU Directives

Deputy Marie Sherlock  My question concerns lipoedema. It is a condition experienced by an estimated 11% of women in this country. We understand a working group was set up in 2023 and was due to report in the second quarter of this year. We have not seen it yet. When will we see that report? I raise significant concern about the outcome of the report, in that the HSE has indicated it relates only to diagnosis and non-surgical management, when we understand surgery should be an important part of the care of these women.

Deputy Jennifer Carroll MacNeill  I propose to take Questions Nos. 98 and 108 together.  I recognise that lipoedema is a chronic and often very painful condition, which can significantly affect physical health, mobility, quality of life and psychological well-being. It is frequently under-recognised or misdiagnosed. I am very aware of the distress many women experience in seeking recognition, diagnosis and appropriate support. Diagnosis remains challenging as there is no specific diagnostic test. It is based on clinical history and examination by an experienced clinician, with investigations used to exclude other conditions or contributing factors. There is currently no medication or cure as such that we are aware of. Management that appears to be the approach, requiring a holistic, multidisciplinary and person-centred approach.  In relation to surgery, the HSE's chief clinical officer has tasked the national clinical programme for plastic surgery with reviewing the evidence. I understand the clinical programme has provided its advice to the chief clinical officer for consideration. I would like to have the opportunity to discuss this with the chief clinical officer in greater detail. I have not had that opportunity in the context of the parliamentary questions and everything else going on. International research continues to evolve, including the LIPLEG trial in Germany examining whether liposuction can provide greater long-term improvements in pain and symptoms compared with complex decongestive therapy alone. That evidence will be important to inform any future updates to the guidance. A working group was established in 2023 to develop a standardised, evidence-based approach for health services but I really want to discuss the matter with the chief clinical officer, recognising the evolving evidence.

Deputy Marie Sherlock  Many in the medical community with expertise in this area are of the view that specialised sparing liposuction surgery - there is big distinction from other types of cosmetic liposuction - is the only intervention that can directly address the pathological adipose tissue issues. Many women have to travel to Cyprus and other countries to access those surgical interventions. Serious consideration has to be given to that surgical intervention in Ireland. Some women who believe they have this condition are seeking liposuction in a setting not specific to lipoedema, which is very worrying. We need to make sure we can provide for these women in this country.

Deputy Maeve O'Connell  I take this opportunity to raise awareness about lipoedema. As Deputy Sherlock said, one in 11 women or girls has it. That is one member of your camogie or football team or two in your leaving certificate class, at least. That is how significant this is. I know the Minister is a huge advocate for women's healthcare. I joined her yesterday in Tallaght hospital for the national women's strategy. We really need to see a strategy and path forward for how we will raise awareness among the public and medical professionals, specifically for early diagnosis, which is so important for early treatment and in preventing this condition from becoming a more severe chronic disease. There are also psychological issues associated with chronic disease, in particular one like lipoedema, which is a physically debilitating and visual disease that women and young girls in particular are very sensitive to.

Deputy Jennifer Carroll MacNeill  I thank both Deputies. It is wonderful that so many other Deputies have joined us to hear that lipoedema affects 11% of women. It is a serious chronic disease and, as with many other conditions for women, we have to think carefully about the research available. Yesterday, we launched the third iteration of the women's health action plan. In that we talk about so many conditions that present differently for women. For example, heart failure in women has different symptoms, has been under-researched and under-diagnosed and treatment outcomes have not been as good because of that.  We put €2 million in funding this year specifically into women's health research, precisely to identify areas that have been under-researched. Many colleagues here have talked about endometriosis. Lipoedema is just as important. It affects 10% or 11% of women and the impact on lymphedema - the crossover that can happen - is also a significant risk with serious health implications.  Women's health has been under-researched and under-diagnosed and there has been a bias in research against understanding women's health as something distinct, important and separate from health generally. We have seen that consistently in the research. It is important we take the opportunity to consider the most contemporary research on lipoedema and that it has the opportunity to feed in at every level. I will discuss it with the chief clinical officer to make sure we have all the best evidence about this condition.  Like the Deputies, I know women with lipoedema. There are many of them. Like the Deputies, I want to advocate loudly for women's health generally. I thank them for joining us at the women's health action plan launch yesterday. We must continue to advocate separately and differently against the bias that has existed against women's health and women's health research for so long. I thank Deputies Sherlock and O'Connell for raising the important issue of lipoedema in front of all Deputies today.
Is féidir teacht ar Cheisteanna Scríofa ar www.oireachtas.ie.
Written Answers are published on the Oireachtas website.
Healthcare Policy

Deputy Eoin Ó Broin  The abuse and trauma suffered by women and children at Bessborough Mother and Baby Home in Cork city is not a matter of history. That suffering is hardwired into the present day lives of survivors and their families. It also echoes in the memory of those who never made it beyond the high walls and wrought iron gates.  The decision to grant planning permission to a private developer to build apartments on the site of the home was appalling, utterly disgraceful. Understandably, it was met with real anger, disgust and hurt by survivors, their families and the public at large, not least because 829 children who died in Bessborough are unaccounted for to this day. We still do not know where they are buried or what happened to them and there has been no proper investigation of the land. It goes against basic decency and compassion to pave over the Bessborough site for any reason, especially while those questions remain unanswered.  Campaign groups have made it clear that they will fight against this decision tooth and nail. It was no surprise that appeals were lodged by survivors to stop the development going ahead. In that time, they have repeatedly called on the Government to buy the Bessborough site to ensure a proper excavation and investigation of the land takes place and, crucially, to preserve Bessborough as a site of national conscience.  Last week, the Taoiseach told the Dáil the Government would await An Coimisiún Pleanála's decision on the appeals. That day has arrived. Today, having already wrongly refused demands for an oral hearing, An Coimisiún Pleanála has now decided to allow the development to proceed, almost in its entirety. As we stand here, Bessborough is to be turned into a construction site, paved over for this development. It is a shameful decision and the Government should simply never have allowed it to get to this point. This is a site whose soil holds many secrets and much trauma and which is potentially the final resting place of hundreds of children. An Coimisiún Pleanála has now made its decision so the question is: what will the Government do? Will it stand back and allow the site, which possibly holds the remains of these children, to be destroyed? Will it try to remove survivors when they chain themselves to the gates in protest, as they have said they will, or will it finally listen to them and hear their calls for respect, justice and truth? Will it buy the Bessborough site and stop this outrage from happening?  The children and women of Bessborough deserved more in life and they were failed, abused in the most horrific fashion. The least the Government should do now is protect what is possibly the final resting place of so many; protect the site so survivors can get the answers, truth and justice they desperately need and deserve; and give these children and women the dignity in death that was stolen from them in life. Those who suffered so much at Bessborough mattered. The women mattered, the children mattered, those who died mattered and the survivors continue to matter.  Will the Minister show survivors and their families that they matter to his Government? Will he give them a clear commitment today that the Government will buy the site, facilitate proper investigation of the land and preserve Bessborough as a site of national conscience?

Minister for Enterprise, Tourism and Employment (Deputy Peter Burke)  I thank Deputy Ó Broin for raising this important matter. I cannot imagine the trauma many families are going through right now as a result of the conclusion of An Coimisiún Pleanála on this file. This was a dark time in our past. Many families were affected by institutions in our State at a time when the State turned a blind eye to vulnerable families - those who needed support at key junctures and crisis points in their lives.  The Government can now engage with survivors and people in the vicinity directly affected by this decision. There is a huge body of work in doing that and we have to do it respectfully and with dignity. In terms of the planning authority and the coimisiún, we know these are independent processes and they have to run their course. The coimisiún came forward with its determination, which was announced this morning.  This is a 60-acre site on a wider 200-acre site where the estate was originally. The Deputy raised a number of items and, as a Government Minister, I cannot respond with precision about what the State will do about a CPO, which the Deputy has proposed, and collectively in terms of a monument for the future. What I would say is it should be victim-centred. Those who have been affected by this trauma and the families who carry this grief to this day need to be front and centre in the determination of the future of that site. I believe that, having spoken to many families about their experiences of this difficult trauma in many different parts of the country. It was horrific and they live consistently with the scars of it.  The Government has done a number of things to try to respond to the challenge of the past - the neglect by the State in the past. We set out in our action plan for survivors and former residents of mother and baby home institutions a number of actions. In November 2022, the then Minister for housing wrote to local authorities, including Cork City Council, requesting adequate consideration be brought to bear in development plan processes and consideration be given to very sensitive sites such as this one. That is key here. At a time when people are vulnerable, people are being reminded of, and consistently endure, the scars of the past. That needs to be brought to bear and brought to the forefront. The Government needs to give them a compassionate hand and work with them to try to get agreement about the future of the site. Notwithstanding that, two independent processes have taken place but I hope the Government will now engage proactively with the victims.  As a father of two young children and as a husband, my heart is absolutely with victims and survivors who had backs turned on them at a hard and dark time in our State. Now we are in a different place.  We are a more compassionate Government. We are an understanding Government. We are a Government that wants to hold out the hand of friendship to try to rectify the scars of the past.

Deputy Eoin Ó Broin  I welcome the Minister's acceptance that the survivors and their families need compassion. However, what they need more urgently is a decision from Government. They have waited long enough and have fought far too hard to get where they are. Their ask is very clear. There is no ambiguity as to what they want. They do not want this building to go ahead. They want the State to acquire the site. They want a proper investigation and excavation of the land. Crucially, they want the site preserved as a site of national conscience. When will the Taoiseach, the Tánaiste and the Government meet the survivors to discuss these issues? When is the Government going to make a decision? Can the Minister at least tell us today that it is the Government's view that this development should not proceed and that the interests of the survivors, their families and those whose bodies may lie in this land should be put before any development, any developer or any profit? When will that meeting take place? When will a decision be made? Should the building not now proceed?

Deputy Peter Burke  This is a time to listen. It is a time to listen to the victims, to understand their trauma, to give them the hand of friendship and to work with them on the future of this site. I do not want to say anything or talk about independent reports or authorities that would exacerbate their trauma. We have to work with victims and survivors. The Government will compassionately engage with survivors in the area. I have not spoken with the Taoiseach or the Tánaiste subsequent to the issuing of the decision by An Coimisiún Pleanála. That work will commence across government. The Government has been very much aware of this issue. It is aware of the processes that were going on. It is obviously very difficult to engage when independent institutions are making a determination as part of a legal process. We now have that opportunity. We will engage proactively with victims and survivors. I say that genuinely as someone who really understands the trauma that many families have experienced at the hands of the institutions of this State going back decades.

Deputy Ged Nash  Another week has gone by and there is still no sign of the report on Aughinish Alumina from the Minister's Department. It is now over three months since The Irish Times published its investigation on 24 March. It is a month since Deputy Bacik asked the Taoiseach to bring forward a solution to protect jobs and to put in place a ban on exports to Russia. It is eight days since President Zelenskyy delivered a hard truth, which is that every tonne of raw materials that ends up in Russia is used in the war against Ukraine.  The evidence in the public domain is damning. The report in The Irish Times clearly showed that, based on official national statistics, over 80% of alumina from Ireland was being shipped to Russia and turned into weapons. The Minister's Department has disputed this. It says the percentage is less than 50%. Even 1% is 1% too much. Let us remember Zelenskyy's words. He said that every tonne is being used against Ukraine. That alumina is killing innocent Ukrainians every single day. Apartment blocks are being indiscriminately hit by Russian missiles and drones made with Irish materials. This is seriously damaging to our international reputation. We are complicit.  Yesterday, the European Parliament voted in favour of a proposal to ban alumina exports to Russia. This amendment was put forward by the Labour Party's S and D group colleagues and the Minister's party's own grouping, the European People's Party. We welcome Fine Gael and Sinn Féin's support for an export ban. It is disturbing that Independent Ireland's MEP voted in support of the continued export of materials of murder. After all of their strong words of support for Ukraine over many years, it is deeply concerning that Fianna Fáil MEPs abstained on a non-binding motion. The Minister's party's MEP took the morally correct position that we need a ban on these exports to Russia. Does the Minister support them? It looks to me as if Fine Gael and Fianna Fáil are divided on this critical issue.  As Labour has said for months, there are options open to us to stop these lethal exports while also protecting jobs. The plant must be nationalised. We will support the Government in taking strong action that sends out an important message internationally as to where we stand. However, the Tánaiste said last week that he had not been part of any discussions about a possible nationalisation of the refinery. That is an astonishing statement to make. We are constantly being told that this operation is important to Ireland as an employer and an energy supplier and that it is vital to the EU industry supply chain. If that is the case, it is an absolute dereliction of duty if the Taoiseach, the Tánaiste and the Minister's Department are not preparing plans with the European Commission to temporarily take over ownership of this facility, especially when Sweden has determined that the parent company is still controlled by a sanctioned Russian billionaire.  I have three simple questions for the Minister. When will the investigation be completed and the report published? Is there political division between the Government parties over Aughinish Alumina? Has the Minister's Department drawn up any plans, contingency or otherwise, to nationalise the Aughinish refinery?

Deputy Peter Burke  I thank Deputy Nash for his question on this very important issue. I will say clearly and unambiguously that I share his concerns in relation to ensuring that no product produced in Ireland ends up in the Russian military complex. We are very firm in that belief. Critically, I say that in the context of a Government that has been unwavering in its support for the people of Ukraine. It has provided almost €500 million in aid and supports Ukraine at the coalface when it needs spare parts for its grid or humanitarian aid and support. The Government has fought for Ukraine in every international forum at which we have speaking rights. Critically, Ireland has provided a home for many Ukrainian citizens and provided 14,000 school places for Ukrainian children in the space of a number of months. Irish society really responded to that challenge.  In connection with the report my Department is preparing, there are three competent authorities licensed to carry out these investigations: the Central Bank, the Department of Foreign Affairs and Trade and the Department itself. My Department has operational responsibility for policy in relation to the EU, along with the Department of Foreign Affairs and Trade. We are preparing this report on foot of that responsibility.  I will put one thing very clearly on record in response to something I have heard repeated on a number of occasions. During the negotiations on the 20 sanctions packages introduced since the war in Ukraine began, our Government has never asked for any sanctions not to be imposed on Aughinish Alumina. We have never asked for any leeway. We have never lobbied on the company's behalf. I have heard suggestions in some outlets that the Irish Government was in that company's corner at key European forums. That is not true. The European Commission has brought forward 20 sanctions packages and Ireland has supported them all 100%. It is very important to put that on the record.  I expect to have the report within the next ten days. At this point in time, we are working on a number of clarifications with the Ukrainian authorities. I met with the Ukrainian ambassador earlier this week. We have also been engaged with the Swedish authorities in connection with their investigations. We need some clarifications. We have to reach a certain evidential threshold before going to the European Commission. That is very challenging. I have been honest and open about that. It will be challenging to get evidence that meets that threshold. I have also said that the Revenue Commissioners are reassessing their figures because the management of Aughinish Alumina has advised that an error was made in recording the trade flows to the EU versus the trade flows to Russia. As I have pointed out in the House previously, it is my understanding that, in 2025, about 44% of exports went to Russia. I absolutely agree that it is unacceptable for any amount to get into the wrong hands. However, I must also be clear that we have to put the facts on the record. I have to meet an evidential threshold. My Department has to stress-test what is in front of it. I am hopeful that it will come to me with the final results of the investigation within the next ten days.

Deputy Ged Nash  The Russians are not using alumina from Aughinish to make Ladas. It is as simple as that. If it is 1%, that is 1% too much. We can argue whether the data says 80% or 50% but, if any scintilla of material from Aughinish is going to the Russian war effort in its illegal war on Ukraine, Ireland needs to stand up. We cannot be selective in the kind of solidarity we show to Ukraine and its civilian population. We need to back Ukraine 100%.  The Minister recently said on "Morning Ireland" that he had no information showing that exports from Aughinish are being used to kill Ukrainian civilians. At this point in time, given that the investigation has been up and running a number of weeks or so now, it is hard to accept that there is no evidence that the Minister is prepared to put on the public record that Aughinish Alumina is, in fact, exporting materials to Russia that are being used for the military effort and to assist the Russian military complex.  It is a little bit hard to take and accept at this point in time, given that the investigation has been up and running for a number of weeks or so now, that there is not any evidence the Minister is prepared to put on the public record that Aughinish Alumina is, in fact, exporting materials to Russia that are being used for the military effort and to assist the Russian military complex. The Minister needs to lay that out here and this report needs to be published before the Dáil rises next Thursday so we can properly debate the report and ensure we are showing the type of real practical support to the people of Ukraine that we ought to be providing.

Deputy Peter Burke  First of all, I was very clear that any amount of alumina getting into the wrong hands is unacceptable. Second, this is an independent report being carried out by my officials. I am not going to go and tell them, when they tell me they are not finished and that they need more time to stress-test the evidence that is in front of them, that they have to finish that report tomorrow. That is interference and I do not want to interfere or prejudice a report on which they are doing their due diligence. They are a competent authority designated under the European Commission and they are experienced to do that work. That work must be completed and then the report will be presented to me. I and the Government have been very clear that our support for Ukraine is unwavering, but we have to get our evidence together. I said that and I was very honest about that. The reporters from the Organized Crime and Corruption Reporting Project, OCCRP, were also very honest when they stated in their report that there is no direct evidence that alumina is getting into Russian military items. We are working so hard to get the direct evidence to ascertain that, but it is challenging. I would be dishonest if I were to say this was going to be a simple solution. This is going to be very complex and, critically, the European Commission will have to stand up and take the lead on this. I have said that we will back it. We have backed the European Commission on every single sanctions package that has come forward. Contrary to reports, we have not lobbied on behalf of Aughinish Alumina, nor have I ever done so as a Minister. I mean to support international rules-based order and the vulnerable people in Ukraine.

Deputy Ruth Coppinger  There have been two femicides this week in Ireland, a femicide being the killing of a woman because she is a woman. The femicide count in this country has officially surpassed the total of last year and it is only July. We send our solidarity to the loved ones of Adina Constantin and Jamey Carney.  Yesterday, the Taoiseach said that figures on violence against women are going in the wrong direction, but what has the Government actually done about them? The most basic thing the Government could do is properly fund the organisations that deal with violence against women. Instead, they are charities. These charities have seen rocketing calls. Women's Aid saw an 11.5% rise last year. There were 62,275 disclosures. It got €1.3 million from the Government. It had to raise €2.7 million in donations. Dublin Rape Crisis Centre, DRCC, had the highest number of calls since its foundation. It got €3.3 million and had to fundraise €1.8 million itself. Does that really strike the Minister as taking gender-based violence seriously when people have to rely on 5 km runs and bag packs to function? If we compare this with the hospitality VAT cut of €680 million or the €25 million for the greyhound industry, it speaks volumes.  The UN has said about gender-related killings, "Initiatives that focus on ... prevention, changing social norms, and engaging whole communities to create zero tolerance for violence against women, work best". This society has a very high tolerance for violence against women: rape allegations against multiple footballers playing in the World Cup, and they play on; the US ambassador going to Conor McGregor's pub, somebody found liable in a civil case for rape; the Taoiseach honouring Trump, a predator, and not listening to survivors when they ask about legislative change.  When is the Minister going to tackle the violent misogyny being spread by Internet companies? The rise in violent misogyny has run parallel to the rise in the statistics for violence against women. Will the Government contact mainstream media outlets that yesterday irresponsibly stoked up the flames of racism by reporting that an asylum seeker was being sought for the murder? The common denominator in violence against women is men, not a nationality. Since Women's Aid has been tracking femicide, it has found that 88% of women were killed by somebody known to them. That includes sons and grandsons. There is a figure from the UK - a mother is more likely to be killed by her own son than by a stranger. Violence against women rocketed during Covid. Why? Because women were stuck in their own homes with their own partners. That disputes any notion that this is anything to do with strangers, asylum seekers or whatever. It became known as a shadow pandemic. What is this Government doing about the pandemic of gender-based violence? It does not strike me that it is treating it as the public health emergency that it is.
Deputy Conor Sheehan  Hear, hear.

Deputy Peter Burke  Absolutely, I concur with the Deputy on the serious nature of femicide and sexual and gender-based violence. That is why the Government does treat this issue with the utmost seriousness. My sympathies go out to any family who have been affected by this. The pain and trauma of victims who have to live with the scars of the actions of others is unimaginable.  First, I will put on the record that there is about €80 million under budget 2026 to support this sector. We have really worked to increase the number of domestic violence units, which is up 30% from 2022, and the number of safe houses, which really needs a huge amount of additional work. I would accept that there are a lot of gaps right across the country that we have to fill. The Minister, Deputy McEntee, took a huge leadership role in this area in the previous Government, which is being carried on by the Minister, Deputy O'Callaghan, in the current Government.  If we look at the actions that have happened, we have our third national zero-tolerance strategy, which now has an implementation group. The implementation group published its report recently. We established Cuan, which is the domestic, sexual and gender-based violence agency taking leadership and accountability in this area. We have very significant evidence from the Central Statistics Office, CSO, Sexual Violence Survey 2022 and we are planning for a second one next year. We have delivered a huge number of awareness campaigns as well.  More importantly, we might look at the legislative change the Government has embarked upon. We have enacted the Family Courts Bill, establishing a dedicated family court to look at very sensitive items for victims. We have introduced an offence for non-fatal strangulation, reflecting the seriousness of the abuse and the stress and harm it causes. The Guardianship of Infants and Child Care (Amendment) Bill 2026 will allow for the removal of guardianship rights from individuals convicted of killing their intimate partner, ensuring stronger protection for children. The Criminal Law (Sexual Offences, Domestic Violence and International Instruments) Bill 2025 will strengthen the legislation around consent and the Criminal Law, Civil Law and Defence (Miscellaneous Provisions) Bill 2026 will restrict the disclosure of counselling records in sexual offence trials and criminalise exploitative practices of sex in lieu of rent. These are key reforms we have embarked upon, as well as the Domestic Violence (Judgments) Register Bill 2026, which will set up a domestic violence register of judgments, ensuring that relevant convictions for serious domestic violence offences are made public as part of the conviction process.  That demonstrates a Government that really treats this issue with the utmost seriousness. More resources need to go into this sector - I absolutely appreciate that - with more support for victims, but the work we are doing demonstrates the Government's commitment to this sector.

Deputy Ruth Coppinger  The Minister said there should be more resources. Why are there not? There is a budget surplus of €10 billion to €12 billion and the Minister is boasting, or saying, that it is sufficient to give €18 million to Cuan, which is the same as the racehorse industry gets. We have seen that this is a public health emergency. The rape crisis services should not be charities. They should be public services in our health service. Why should people have to go bag packing for a 24-hour helpline or whatever it is? The Too Into You campaign, for example, something I used myself when I was teaching, is funded as part of that €1.3 million. Why is that not being rolled out in every single workplace and school, educating people about toxic relationships? We could prevent femicide happening if we did that. Recently, the topic of counselling notes was a massive issue for survivors of gender violence. The Minister for justice utterly failed to talk to the rape crisis centre about its proposals or to listen to what survivors had to say. It did not reflect very well at all. It was a very upsetting experience for those people. The latest victim of femicide is being victim-blamed for her own death thanks to media outlets that irresponsibly stoke racism. That should be tackled by this Government as well.

Deputy Peter Burke  I would be careful about comparing such a serious issue with other sectors of the economy that are getting money.  This is a really serious issue that deserves the utmost attention of Government. I have demonstrated through three strategies and through the increase in resources to €80 million under budget 2026 the acceleration of the number of refuges for women and of safe houses. I acknowledge we do not have enough.
Deputy Ruth Coppinger  It is terrible.

Deputy Peter Burke  I am being very honest about that but we are we are putting more resources into it. I also pointed out the legislative reform we have seen, critically increasing maximum sentences for assault causing harm to ten years and defending the rights of children who are left behind in very horrific circumstances through femicide or where someone kills their spouse or partner. Those areas are key parts of our reforms. We are demonstrating that we take this very seriously. We want to support women and that is why Cuan was established working together with Government and the CSO to get the data to establish what we can best do over the years ahead. We have an implementation group for that strategy. We want to be there as a compassionate Government to stand by women and to stand by victims of domestic, sexual and gender-based violence.

Deputy Michael Fitzmaurice  Reference was made earlier to Independent Ireland and a vote in the European Parliament. I want to put on the record that Independent Ireland issued a statement to make it very clear that we stand with the people in Limerick and surrounding areas who have large mortgages and young families. The facts are not out yet on the percentages one way or the other. I want that on the record rather than innuendo being spread.  Every one of us here has probably had contact with the National Rehabilitation Hospital. It does great work to bring people, who were involved in accidents, suffer strokes or have different illnesses, back to full health. It is to be commended on what it has done down through the years for many families. Phase 1 of its project was finished in 2020. It was actually brought in on budget and has been a huge success. Phase 2 is to bring add-on parts of it all together in a new build. Phase 3 will increase the number of beds from something like 125 to 220 or 230.  One of the Minister's party colleagues announced in 2018 that Roscommon would get a specialised 20-bed unit to cover the west and north west. There are satellite units in Mayo, Galway and other counties throughout the country. While the huge work being done there is acknowledged, it is widely known that there is basically a waiting list of a minimum of six months, which, for people who need the help and the expertise the National Rehabilitation Hospital gives, can be a long time waiting. In some cases if people do not get in in time, it may have an adverse effect. When will the Government put the capital into phase 2 and phase 3? For the specialised unit to cover the west and the north west, which was announced eight years ago, where are we at in making sure that it will complement the National Rehabilitation Hospital here in Dublin and take pressure off it? When will the beds be supplied in phase 3 here in Dublin?

Deputy Peter Burke  I thank Deputy Fitzmaurice for a very important and timely question on the National Rehabilitation Hospital. From our constituency clinics and working in our community, we all know how important a piece in the jigsaw of rehabilitation the National Rehabilitation Hospital is. Critically what really stands out is the work that can be carried out for patients with early intervention after accidents, acquired brain injuries or traumatic experiences. We are all very aware phase 1 was opened three years after construction commenced in October 2017. The facility represented a €129 million investment on behalf of the State. Phase 2 will provide 120 new beds and provide additional outreach to Deputy Fitzmaurice's constituency and other constituencies around the country, which will be very welcome and very much supported.  I understand that phase 2 proposals are estimated to cost more than €200 million. A strategic assessment is currently being prepared in accordance with the Government's infrastructure guidelines. The Deputy will know of decisions taken over the past number of weeks to speed up capital infrastructure. We will deliver €175 billion in infrastructure over the next decade. It is critical that the Government can carry out the assessments that underpin that delivery on a timely basis. The assessment is being prepared under the revised infrastructure guidelines. I am not aware that the Department of Health has received the final assessment to give it the go-ahead as of yet. I am very much aware of this and I will bring the Deputy's comments to the Minister for Health. The National Rehabilitation Hospital has been an important a piece in the jigsaw for so many patients who find themselves there as a result of an accident, illness, injury, or physical or cognitive disability. It has been critical in their rehabilitation and in many cases bringing them back to functioning in society again, which is so important.  Under the investment plan, the Department of Health has a record capital allocation to deliver a world-class health system, supporting citizens right across the country. We have a lot of work to do and the NRH would be very much to the forefront of my mind. I will make the Minister, Deputy Carroll MacNeill, aware of the Deputy's concerns. I hope that under the new revised infrastructure guidelines the report will be delivered in a timely manner and critically that we can get on with delivering phase 2 of this much-needed facility.

Deputy Michael Fitzmaurice  I thank the Minister. However, there is one thing we need to know. It is important the Department of Health and indeed the Department of public expenditure engage with the people involved in the National Rehabilitation Hospital. We frequently hear about assessments. As the Minister is aware, the reality that it takes six months for some people to get in the door to get on the road to recovery. While initially they may be in step-down services trying to help them as best they can, they need the expertise of the National Rehabilitation Hospital. When will phase 2 be funded and started? I think all the parts of it are done, bar funding. When will the specialised unit be put in place in Roscommon to serve the people in the west and the north west?

Deputy Peter Burke  I would see that as a matter of urgency. In tandem, the Department of Health is currently preparing a business case, which has to be done through the infrastructure guidelines. As the Deputy quite rightly points out, the improvements that the facility can make for people with early intervention have been demonstrated. That is critical. I have seen the work the hospital does, particularly for those who have been involved in very traumatic experiences. I will raise the Deputy's concern in relation to outreach in Roscommon with the Minister for Health. I would be confident that this decision will be made quickly. It should not be about funding. We have a very significant war chest of €175 billion over the next decade to deliver public infrastructure. The critical thing - the Government has experienced this - is getting the money spent, getting it into the bricks and mortar and ensuring that we can get it through the process quickly. One thing we have learned over the past number of years is that we have to get a lot better at delivering public infrastructure. We have set the guide rails to do that. We will spend about €19.1 billion this year, an increase of 54% in two years, which demonstrates the real ambition of the Government.
Ceisteanna ó Cheannairí - Leaders' Questions

debateSection.showAs: Ceisteanna ó na Comhaltaí Eile - Other Members’ Questions

text.text: Deputy Michael Murphy  I am immensely proud to represent the constituency of Tipperary South, a constituency blessed with extraordinary strengths, great history, strong employment, great outdoors, an outstanding tourism offering, wonderful retail and hospitality businesses, and above all extraordinary community spirit. Of course, like every constituency we have our challenges. We continue to tackle vacancy and dereliction. We continue to fight for investment particularly in our water and wastewater infrastructure.  However, today I want to raise what many constituents tell me is their greatest concern, the growing feeling that law and order is slipping away.  Across many of our key towns, including Clonmel, Tipperary, Carrick, Cahir, Fethard and Cashel, people are telling me they no longer feel safe. Businesses are witnessing open drug dealing in prime retail areas, families are increasingly concerned about antisocial behaviour and criminal gangs operate with increased confidence. At the same time, Garda visibility has reduced dramatically. Response times are far too long and in some instances people struggle to even make contact with their local Garda station. One constituent recently attempted to contact Clonmel Garda station more than 20 times during a serious incident without receiving an answer. Increasingly, people believe the only way of getting assistance is by dialling 999. I want to be absolutely clear: this is not a criticism of the men and women of An Garda Síochána serving on the ground in my constituency. Every interaction I have with them tells me they are working tirelessly, but under enormous pressure.  The problem is numbers. The programme for Government rightly speaks about balanced regional development, yet when it comes to Garda resources, there is nothing balanced about the current approach. This year, 356 recruits graduated from Templemore over two intakes, and just two came to the entire county of Tipperary. By comparison, 245, or more than 70%, went to the Dublin metropolitan region. When retirements and transfers are taken into account, Tipperary has effectively experienced a net loss. Since the division restructuring was introduced in 2020, Garda numbers across the Tipperary-Clare division have fallen from 725 to 660. Having the chief superintendent based in Ennis is simply not delivering for the communities I represent.  Will the Government commit to urgently reviewing Garda allocations for counties like Tipperary? Does the Minister support the review of the Clare-Tipperary divisional structure to ensure policing resources genuinely reflect the need of our communities?

Minister for Enterprise, Tourism and Employment (Deputy Peter Burke)  I thank Deputy Murphy for his very important question, articulating the challenges to his constituency and standing up for his constituents.  The resource base the Government has allocated to An Garda Síochána in 2026, particularly through the justice budget, is €2.74 million, which is up 22% since 2020. There has been a significant increase in capital that we have deployed to ensure all corners of our country are supported in the context of justice and law and order, which is very close to all our hearts.  The increase in civilian staff within An Garda Síochána has freed up about 900 front-line gardaí. That should act as an accelerant to ensure front-line gardaí are freed up to work and be on the beat on the streets, while civilian staff can fill the gaps in other areas. If we keep going at this rate of recruits being attested through Templemore, we will be on target to surpass the all-time record number of gardaí, which will be very important. This is a strong demonstration of how that commitment needs to be held in communities right across our country.  The overall Garda workforce had increased from 15,980 in 2016 to 18,577 as of 31 May. Critically, the Government has made significant changes to recruitment, including the age limit, the training allowance, the shortening of recruitment timeframes, reform of the physical competency test and the introduction of an assessment of care approach to elements of the recruitment process. Two recruitment drives were launched in 2025. There were over 11,000 applications for the first round and in excess of 4,000 for the second. That shows we have a very strong pipeline to get recruits attested through Templemore, which is very important. Obviously, there was a significant lag to get the Government to build that up again and get going in shortening the process. However, getting Templemore operating at maximum capacity with a much shorter process will assist significantly with this.  As regards the Clare-Tipperary division, as the Deputy quite rightly pointed out, there has been a challenge. About 660 gardaí are assigned to that division and, obviously, for the record, it is the Commissioner who has operational responsibility for deciding where gardaí serve in particular districts. However, there are significant incentives for gardaí to move, which is facilitated through the new rules and should help people to return close to their areas. This should help rural communities like the Deputy's.  We will absolutely bring the Deputy's concerns to the attention of the Minister for justice and continue to work with the Deputy in fighting for his constituents and standing up for Tipperary.

Deputy Michael Murphy  This is about fairness. Just two gardaí have been allocated to Tipperary this year, with 245 going to the Dublin metropolitan region. Criminal gangs are operating with increased confidence in my constituency. Our gardaí on the ground in south Tipperary deserve reinforcement. Our communities deserve reassurance and our businesses deserve to operate without open criminality undermining our town centres. The reputation of south Tipperary is only as strong as the people's sense of safety. Once that begins to disappear, everything else is put at risk.  Will the Minister commit to organising a meeting for all Oireachtas Members in south Tipperary - I note Deputy Healy is in the room, as well as Deputy McGrath - in order to review the allocation model, particularly as it applies to south Tipperary, and more importantly, to explore a review of the Clare-Tipperary divisional structure, which is not serving the people I represent?

Deputy Peter Burke  I thank Deputy Murphy for being proactive and bringing forward a solution to try to operationalise a group of the Oireachtas and showing leadership to do that for Tipperary. I will absolutely bring that to the attention of the Minister for justice, particularly considering the cross-party aspect. This is very important and shows leadership. Essentially, the Deputy is trying to deliver for his community.  Retail crime has been a big challenge and is an area on which my colleague the Minister of State, Deputy Dillon, has done a huge amount of work to support retailers, which has been very challenging over the last number of years. Critically, as the Deputy pointed out, the increase in the recruitment model is not enough for Tipperary. We will do everything we can as a Government to support and ensure rural communities are safe. One thing I am very clear on as a member of Government is that you cannot tell people they feel safe; they must feel it themselves. They must be very much aware of the resources being put in and see their gardaí as active. The Government is doing everything to increase that pipeline to ensure it is responding at a time when we have the resources to protect local communities like the Deputy's, so I will absolutely carry forward his request.
Ceisteanna ó na Comhaltaí Eile - Other Members’ Questions

debateSection.showAs: Ceisteanna ar Pholasaí nó ar Reachtaíocht - Questions on Policy or Legislation

text.text: Deputy Matt Carthy  There is chaos in our courts as a result of the justice Minister's mishandling of the criminal legal aid scheme review. We now see solicitors resigning from the criminal legal aid panel, increasing numbers of cases being adjourned, people going without legal representation and others with no legal expertise attempting to represent themselves. This is a criminal justice system on the brink of collapse, and the justice Minister seems entirely satisfied to let this continue.  Will the Government allow this to continue or will it intervene to ensure the complexity of this issue is recognised? Will it suspend the changes made by the Minister, engage in real consultation with all stakeholders and deliver a criminal legal aid scheme that is efficient for court services, prudent with public finances and has the confidence of the Irish people?

Minister for Enterprise, Tourism and Employment (Deputy Peter Burke)  I am very much aware of how important solicitors are to our criminal justice system and our constitutional right to representation, which is the undercurrent of any constitutional democracy. Every Government has to reform, however, and it is key that in working with the Minister for justice and having dialogue with the parties concerned, we get an agreement here. The Government has to be very clear. If we look at the data over the last number of years, there has been a 115% increase in fees in the criminal justice system, yet the number of cases has reduced.  We have to tackle the issues with reform in that regard. I am sure the Deputy would support that when he sees data like that. Critically, we have countered the current offer-----
Deputy Matt Carthy  The engagement has not taken place.

Deputy Peter Burke  -----of it equating to five appearances in the District Court plus an additional 8%. We need to have a landing zone for this as a Government. We need to ensure that gardaí and solicitors can do their jobs and that the criminal justice system can function but a Government has to reform and we cannot be held back from doing that.
Deputy Matt Carthy  Will engagement take place?

Deputy Jen Cummins  I am heartbroken to be standing here today having learned of the disgraceful decision to continue with the plans to build in Bessborough. For 923 children, being born in Bessborough was a death sentence, and the burial sites of 859 of those children have never been found. If this development goes ahead, their remains could be lost forever. I pay tribute to Councillor Noelle Brown and everyone who has been advocating to prevent this from happening. This decision is unfathomable and we need the Government to step up on this. Last week, Deputy Cairns asked the Taoiseach what would happen if it was granted and if it should be compulsorily purchased. He said a decision would be made afterwards. What is that decision? What will the Government do? A decision has been made to grant that permission. Will the Government issue a compulsory purchase order to buy it? I know the Minister said earlier that his heart is broken. We can say that but we have to have action, and we need to make sure that something happens. There needs to be full understanding of what will happen now and how the Government can step in to prevent this from happening.

Deputy Peter Burke  I thank Deputy Cummins for her important question. As I said earlier, this is an important time to support and work with victims. Obviously a legal process was under way with the competent authority, which was An Coimisiún Pleanála, making its determination. Now we need the space to liaise with victims, and to support survivors, which is very important. It would be a huge disservice to them if I was to make a decision on the hoof on the Dáil floor without having the imprimatur of my Government colleagues. It is not my responsibility to do so but I will absolutely raise the concern the Deputy has brought forward with the Taoiseach, the Tánaiste and the respective Minister.

Deputy Mark Wall  Today is No Wait Card Ireland Awareness Day. It is supported by Crohn's and Colitis Ireland, Spinal Injuries Ireland, the Irish Cancer Society and the Continence Foundation of Ireland. I thank Kildare woman Maria Crowe once again for raising the importance of this card. The Minister will know that, with the support of the Labour Part, I have a Bill on Second Stage to make this card widely available and recognised. I take the opportunity to thank the Minister's colleague, the Minister for Health, Deputy Jennifer Carroll MacNeill, for her continued support and advice on this card, and the Chief Medical Officer and her staff. Raising the awareness and acceptance of this card would be a life-changer for hundreds of thousands of Irish people who suffer from bowel and bladder diseases and medical conditions, of which I am one. It would give us all independence and freedom to plan our lives better. I ask the Minister and Government to continue to support the development of such a card and ensure the Minister for Health has all the supports that her Department needs to bring this life-changing card to the hands of those who need it most, giving hope to young and old and changing their lives for good.

Deputy Peter Burke  I thank Deputy Wall for his very important intervention. I heard part of the interview this morning, which was exceptional and very courageous, showing leadership in an area which affects so many people in our society. I know the Minister for Health is supportive of the Deputy's initiative and remains so. I hope that we can all work together to make society a better place for people who struggle with these life-limiting conditions and give them the opportunity to function as normally as they can, notwithstanding the challenges that they have, and make society a better place for us all. The Minister, Deputy Carroll MacNeill, is very supportive. I hope the Minister and Deputy Wall will continue to work together. I commend Deputy Wall on his work and leadership in this regard and the exceptional interview that we heard this morning.

Deputy Seamus Healy  Across Ireland, 624,000 people provide regular unpaid care to children and adults with additional needs. A recent 2026 report on care shows that 75% of carers never received respite, 49% cut back on essentials such as food and heating, and 48% experienced severe loneliness. Of course, these carers saved the State millions of euro annually. I have a specific question arising from a family in my constituency. This family's severely disabled teenaged son passed away last year after a long and traumatic illness. The dad had to give up work but is now in a position to return to work. He is being penalised by way of a reduction of his working family payment because his wife's carer's allowance for another family member is assessed against him. This is deeply unfair and I would ask that the Minister ensure the carer's allowance payment is disregarded for purposes of assessment of the working family payment.

Minister of State at the Department of Children, Disability and Equality (Deputy Emer Higgins)  I ask the Deputy to relay my condolences to his constituent at this very difficult time.
